conversation.message: Honestly, I don't know why I'm even here. I've tried quitting before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I just don't see the point in trying again.
You sound really discouraged about trying to quit again. What was it about those previous attempts that made them feel like they weren't worth trying again?

I don't know... I just couldn't do it. I tried nicotine gum, prescription medication, and even cold turkey. But I always ended up going back to smoking. And it's not just that... I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always been conscious of my weight, and the thought of gaining even a few pounds is unbearable to me.
You've had past experiences with weight gain after quitting, which seems to have been a significant concern for you. How do you think your weight concerns might be connected to your smoking habits?
I'm not sure... I've tried to diet before, and it never works out. And besides, I don't think I'm ready to quit smoking right now. I'm just not in the right mindset.
You've mentioned that you're not in the right mindset to quit smoking right now. Can you tell me more about what that feels like for you?
I suppose... but I'm not making any promises. What kind of small steps are we talking about?
You're not feeling entirely committed to quitting right now, and that's okay. What do you think are the situations or emotions that usually make you reach for a cigarette?
Okay, fine. Let's get this over with. But like I said, I'm not making any promises.

I hear you saying you're not excited about this process, but you're willing to try some small steps. What's going on that's making you feel so resistant to quitting, even if it's just for a little while?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I'm not really interested in quitting. I've tried before, and it never works out. I've been smoking for 20 years, and it's just a part of who I am.

You're feeling a bit stuck about quitting and it's hard to imagine life without cigarettes, which has been a part of your life for 20 years. 

Can you think of a specific situation or moment when you felt most comfortable or relaxed while smoking?

Well, I've tried nicotine gum, patches, and even cold turkey. But every time, I end up feeling anxious and depressed. It's like my mental health gets worse when I try to quit. And on top of that, I always gain weight when I quit, which is really frustrating.

I can understand why quitting would be so frustrating for you, especially with the added stress of feeling anxious and depressed. 

What do you typically turn to when you're feeling anxious or depressed, like you did when trying to quit with the gum, patches, or cold turkey?

Yeah, I've talked to my doctor. They just tell me to quit smoking, like it's that easy. They don't understand how hard it is. And my family and friends don't get it either. They're all smokers, so they just think I should keep smoking and not worry about it.

It sounds like your doctor and some of your loved ones don't fully understand the challenges you're facing with quitting smoking. How do you think that lack of understanding affects your motivation to quit?
I don't know...I just don't think I can do it. I've failed so many times before.

It sounds like all these past failures have left you feeling discouraged and unsure if quitting is even possible. What is it about feeling like a "failure" that's making you doubt your ability to quit?
I...I guess so. But I'm not making any promises.
It sounds like you're willing to consider taking small steps towards quitting, but you're not feeling committed yet. What's holding you back from making a promise to reduce your smoking?

Well, I've been feeling really guilty about smoking around my niece and nephew. They're always asking me why I smell like smoke, and it breaks my heart. I want to be a good role model for them, and I don't want to die young from some smoking-related illness.

It sounds like being around your niece and nephew and their questions about the smoke really bothers you. What do you typically do and where do you usually go when you're feeling guilty about smoking around them?
Hmm...I'd say about a 6? I mean, I've tried before and failed, so I'm not sure if I can really do it this time.
That makes sense, given your past attempts, Rachel. What do you think was the hardest part about quitting the last time you tried?

I think I need more support. My friends and family are all smokers, so it's hard to stay motivated when everyone around me is still smoking. And as a freelancer, I work from home, so I don't have coworkers to support me either.
It sounds like you're feeling isolated in your smoking habit, which can make it harder to quit. What are some typical days like for you, and how does smoking fit into your daily routine?
Yeah, I've thought about it, but I don't know...I'm not really sure where to start or if it would even help.
It sounds like you're feeling unsure about where to start with getting support, Rachel. What are some specific situations or times when you feel like you need support the most?
Yeah, okay. I guess it couldn't hurt to try.
So you're open to trying new resources, that's a great step forward. What do you think might make it hard for you to stick with a support group or counseling, even if it's just for a few sessions?

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit a couple of times before. It didn't work out. I guess I'm just worried about the cost of quitting. I've heard those prescription medications can be really expensive.
You mentioned trying to quit before, but it didn't work out. What specifically happened when you tried to quit that made you go back to smoking?

Well, like I said, the cost is a big deal. I'm not sure if my insurance will cover the medications or the nicotine replacement therapy. And even if it does, I'm not sure if I can afford the copays. And then there's the fear of failure. I've tried to quit before, and I didn't make it. I don't want to disappoint my family and friends again. As for my smoking habits, I usually smoke about a pack a day.

You're feeling anxious about the cost and fear of failure, and that's making it tough for you to take the next step. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about quitting and potentially disappointing your loved ones again?
Okay, that makes sense. I didn't realize my nicotine dependence level was that high. And yeah, let's talk about those options. I want to know what might work best for me.
I'm glad we're exploring the options together. When you're feeling stressed or anxious, which situations or emotions make you reach for a cigarette?
Okay, that sounds good. I think I'd like to try the nicotine replacement therapy. And what about counseling and support groups? How do those work?

You're considering the nicotine replacement therapy, and you're also interested in learning more about counseling and support groups. Can you tell me more about what's going through your mind when you think about quitting smoking and what's making it hard for you to stay motivated?
Yeah, I knew it was bad for me, but I didn't realize it was that bad.
You're really starting to understand the risks of smoking. What situations make you most likely to reach for a cigarette?
Yeah, that sounds like a good plan. I can do that. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I can actually do this this time.
Since you've just set a quit date and are feeling more confident, I'm curious - what do you usually do to cope with stress, since you mentioned it's one of the things that triggers your smoking?
Honestly, doc, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think I need to quit smoking. It's not a big deal. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I'm fine.
You seem to be feeling pretty confident about your smoking habit. What do you think might change your mind about quitting, if anything?
Quitting? Nah, I don't think so. I've tried it before, and it didn't work out. I don't want to gain weight, and I don't want to give up the social aspect of smoking. It's part of who I am, you know?

You mention the social aspect of smoking is important to you, and it feels like a big part of your identity. How do you usually feel when you're around others who are smoking, and do you find that your desire to smoke increases in those situations?
I don't know...I've seen friends who quit and they just ballooned up. I don't want that to happen to me. And what about when I'm out with friends at the bar? Smoking is just part of the experience.
Considering your concerns about weight gain and social situations, have you thought about how you'd handle those situations if you weren't smoking?
It's just...I'm a bartender, and smoking is part of the culture. My friends all smoke, and it's just what we do. If I quit, I'd feel like I'm missing out or something.

I see that smoking is deeply connected to your work and social life as a bartender. Have you ever had a friend try to quit smoking and stick to it for a while, or do you think everyone who quits ultimately goes back to smoking?
I guess so...but I'm still not sure I want to quit.

It sounds like you're still unsure about quitting, but you're open to exploring some alternatives. Can you think of a time when you were able to resist a cigarette in a situation where you normally would have smoked?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit before, but I started again. I don't know why I can't just stay quit. It's like I'm stuck in this cycle.
I can sense how frustrated you're feeling about starting again. Before we dive into what's going on, can you tell me how many cigarettes you usually smoke in a day?

Well, I was doing okay for a while. I had cut down to just a few cigarettes a day, and I was feeling pretty proud of myself. But then I started getting really stressed with work, and I just fell back into old habits. I'd take a break, and the next thing I knew, I was smoking again. It's like my brain just automatically goes to cigarettes when I'm feeling overwhelmed.

It sounds like you were making progress, feeling proud of yourself, but then stress crept in and you fell back into smoking again. How does it make you feel when you think about the fact that you were proud of yourself for cutting down, only to relapse?
Hmm, I don't think I've tried those before. How do they work?

I'm glad we're exploring these options together. What is it about the idea of using nicotine replacement therapy or prescription medications that's resonating with you, and what's holding you back from considering it?
Okay, that sounds like it could be helpful.
What is it about not using nicotine replacement therapy or prescription medications that's making you hesitant, or is there something else that's holding you back from considering them?
Yeah, that would be great.

You're willing to explore some tips on healthy eating and exercise to manage potential weight gain - that's a great step. What's been holding you back from quitting smoking all these years, and what's making you think it's the right time to try again now?
I suppose I could try to cut down to just one cigarette break a day instead of two or three. That might be a start.

You're feeling hopeful that cutting down to one cigarette break a day could be a good start. What makes you think you'll be able to stick to this new goal, and what's holding you back from making bigger changes right now?
Yeah, that sounds good. I think I need all the help I can get right now.

You're feeling really overwhelmed and unsure about how to move forward. Can you tell me, what's driving you to want to quit smoking in the first place - is it concerns about your health, your family, or something else?

Honestly, I'm not doing great. I've been smoking for 20 years, and I've tried to quit a few times, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself. I feel like I should be able to quit, but it's just so hard.
I can sense your frustration with yourself, and that's understandable after 20 years of smoking. What situations or emotions usually make you reach for a cigarette?

It's just...I have depression and anxiety, and smoking seems to help me cope with those feelings. But at the same time, I know it's making things worse. And I've tried to quit before, but I always gain weight, and that just makes me feel even more miserable. And my friends and family all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation. And to top it all off, my partner doesn't support me in my quit attempts, so I feel like I'm doing it all on my own.

It sounds like smoking has become a way for you to cope with your depression and anxiety, but it's also causing more problems. Have you ever thought about writing down why quitting smoking is so important to you, and what benefits you hope to gain by stopping?

Well, when I'm feeling anxious or overwhelmed, smoking just seems to calm me down, you know? It's like a temporary escape from my emotions. But I know it's not a healthy way to deal with things, and it's just making everything worse in the long run.

It sounds like smoking acts as a quick escape for you when you're feeling anxious or overwhelmed. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside when you're feeling that way, to see if it helps calm you down?
Yeah, I guess I never thought about that. I've always just focused on quitting smoking, but maybe I need to focus on dealing with the underlying issues first. That makes sense.

Given the patient's understanding of the connection between their underlying issues and smoking, I'd like to gently introduce a coping strategy. Let's try practicing a simple "no" when offered a cigarette, and reminding yourself why you quit. Would you be open to trying that?

Honestly, I'm not really sure. I mean, I know smoking's bad for me and all, but I've been doing it for years and I'm not really sure I'm ready to quit. Plus, I've got a lot of other stuff going on in my life right now.
It sounds like you're feeling a bit uncertain about quitting smoking right now. Can you tell me how many cigarettes you smoke per day?
I guess I just feel like it's not good for me, and I should probably stop. But like I said, I've got a lot of other stuff going on, and I'm not sure I'm ready to take on quitting smoking too.
How long have you been smoking, Alex?
Yeah, I've heard that before, but I'm not sure I believe it. I mean, I've smoked for years and I'm fine.

Alex, I can see you're not entirely convinced yet, and that's okay. I want to try something that might help you see things differently. Have you ever noticed that when you're feeling stressed, you often reach for a cigarette as a way to calm yourself down, even if you're not even aware of it?
Yeah, I guess so. I'm worried that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse.
You're worried that quitting smoking might affect your medication. Have you talked to your doctor about the potential impact of quitting on your depression and anxiety treatment?
Okay, that sounds like it might be helpful.
You've taken a big step in reaching out for support, Alex – I'm here for you as you continue on this path.
Yeah, I knew that. But like I said, I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit.
I hear your concern about gaining weight, Alex. Would you be open to trying a daily plan to track your cravings and stress levels, and then finding healthy alternatives to manage them?
Okay, I think I'm willing to listen and learn more.

Since you're in the Planning phase, I should focus on suggesting a relevant coping strategy or technique based on the patient's specific triggers and past attempts. 

Alex, given that stress is a known trigger for you, have you ever noticed any specific activities that helped you manage stress while trying to quit smoking in the past?

Honestly, I'm really struggling. I've been smoking for 15 years, and I've tried to quit three times before. The longest I lasted was three months, but I always end up going back to it. I'm just so frustrated with myself.

You've been trying to quit for a while now, and it's clear that it's really tough for you. What specifically do you think triggered your relapse each time, was it stress, certain situations, or something else?

My daughter. She's getting older, and I don't want her to see me smoking. I'm worried about the example I'm setting for her. And to be honest, I'm also worried about my health. I've been experiencing some breathing issues lately, and I know it's related to smoking.
You're really motivated to quit for your daughter's sake and your own health. When you're feeling overwhelmed by those breathing issues, how do you usually cope with the physical discomfort?

Well, that's the thing. I don't really have one. My husband is a social smoker, and my friends all smoke. They don't understand why I can't just cut down instead of quitting altogether. And my work schedule is so busy, I don't have time for support groups or counseling.
Who in your life could be that person for you, someone to call when the urge is strong and who will support your decision to quit without offering you a cigarette?
Yeah, I've been using some online forums and apps, but it's hard to connect with others. It feels like I'm going through this alone. And sometimes the advice they give just doesn't resonate with me.

You've tried online forums and apps before, but it didn't quite work for you. What is it about those experiences that makes you think you need something more personal, something that connects with you on a deeper level?
Cravings. I get these intense cravings, especially during stressful work meetings or when I'm driving. And sometimes I just give in to them.

With all those stressful work meetings and driving situations, it can be really overwhelming. Would you be open to trying a technique where you change your environment when you feel a craving coming on, like stepping outside for some fresh air or moving to a different room?
Yeah, I'm willing to try anything at this point. I just want to quit for good this time.
You've taken a huge step by acknowledging your desire to quit for good – I'm proud of your courage and determination.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two years, but then I started again after a night out with friends. Now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day. It's frustrating because I know I can quit, but it feels like I'm stuck in this cycle.
I can sense your frustration and disappointment about falling back into the smoking habit. What do you think triggered you to start smoking again after quitting for two years?

Well, I was out with friends at a bar, and we were all having a few drinks. It's just so easy to smoke when I'm drinking. I feel like I can relax and be myself around my friends when we're all smoking together. It's like it's a part of who I am when I'm socializing.

It sounds like socializing with friends is a big part of why you feel like smoking is a part of who you are. What is it about those moments with your friends that makes you feel like you need to smoke, and what would it take for you to feel like you could enjoy those moments without smoking?

Honestly, I don't know. I feel like I'd be the only one not smoking, and it would be awkward. Plus, I'm worried that if I quit, I'll just get anxious and irritable, and that would be even worse than smoking.

You're worried about feeling awkward and anxious in social situations if you're not smoking. How would you feel if you had a plan in place for handling those situations, like having a friendly drink in your hand or finding a non-smoking buddy?

I guess it would be better for my health, and I wouldn't have to worry about the smell or the cost. But it's hard to see past the short-term struggles. I just feel like I've failed already, so why bother trying again?

It sounds like you're struggling with feelings of failure after trying to quit before. That can be really tough. How can we use that experience to our advantage this time, and what worked even a little for you the last time you tried to quit?

Look, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think quitting is a big deal. I've been smoking for 25 years, and it's not like it's killing me or anything. I enjoy it, it helps me relax, and it's just part of who I am.

You sound a bit ambivalent about quitting, and I'm here to support you in exploring what's holding you back from making a decision. Can you tell me what might change for you if you were to quit smoking?

Well, like when I'm dealing with a difficult project or client, it calms me down. And when I'm on a break at work or waiting for a job to start, I just smoke out of habit, I guess. And, you know, when I'm with friends or at parties, it makes me feel more relaxed and social. But, honestly, I don't think I can quit. I've tried before, and it didn't work. I don't think I can do it.

Alex, it sounds like you've been using cigarettes as a way to cope with stress and anxiety, especially in social situations. What does it mean to you that you've tried to quit before, but it didn't work?

Oh, I don't know... I guess I'd have more money, and my wife would be off my back about it. But, to be honest, I don't think it would make a huge difference. I've got other issues, like my depression and anxiety, that are way more important to deal with. And, like I said, smoking helps me cope with those things. Plus, I don't think I can quit without quitting drinking and pot too, and I'm not willing to do that.

I hear that you feel smoking is closely linked to managing your depression and anxiety, Alex. Can you tell me more about how you think quitting smoking would impact your ability to cope with those feelings?
I don't know... I'm not sure I believe that. But, I guess it couldn't hurt to talk about it. But, don't expect me to quit anytime soon.

I understand your skepticism about quitting, Alex, and it's okay to have doubts. What's holding you back from even considering that quitting smoking could be an important step towards managing your depression and anxiety?

Honestly, I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed. I've been trying to quit for a while now, but it's just so hard. I've tried cold turkey, nicotine gum, and even prescription medication, but nothing seems to stick. I'm worried that I'll never be able to quit.
I can sense the frustration and worry in your voice. It takes a lot of courage to try quitting like you have. What do you usually do in the moments right before you reach for a cigarette?

Honestly, I'm struggling. I quit about a month ago, but it's been tough. I've been using nicotine gum, but I still find myself thinking about smoking all the time. And to be honest, I'm worried about gaining weight. I've always had a hard time with my weight, and I'm scared that quitting smoking will make it worse.
You mentioned quitting was tough, and nicotine gum didn't work out for you - what usually triggers the urge to smoke for you, like certain situations or emotions?

I guess I just got tired of feeling like a slave to cigarettes. I was smoking 15-20 a day, and it was taking a toll on my health and my wallet. And to be honest, I was getting tired of the smell and the way it made me feel. But it's hard because I really enjoy smoking. I miss the social aspect of it, and I miss the ritual of it. It's hard to imagine relaxing without a cigarette.

You really miss the social aspect of smoking and the ritual of it. That's a tough thing to give up. Have you considered finding alternative ways to socialize and relax that don't involve cigarettes, like going for a walk with friends instead of meeting at a coffee shop?

I think it would be amazing. I would feel so much healthier, and I would be proud of myself for overcoming this addiction. But I'm scared that quitting will make my depression and anxiety worse. I've struggled with those conditions for a while, and I'm worried that quitting smoking will make them harder to manage.
I hear your concerns about depression and anxiety. Have you talked to your doctor or a mental health professional about how quitting smoking might affect these conditions?
Yeah, I think that would be helpful. I just feel like I'm stuck and I don't know how to move forward.

I totally get why you feel stuck - it's like you're at a crossroads and not sure which path to take. Let's think about this: what if we were to plan a scenario for a social situation where you might normally smoke, and we came up with a plan to handle it beforehand?
Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for two weeks six months ago, but I ended up starting again. It's just so hard. I feel like I've failed.

It sounds like you're feeling pretty down on yourself about quitting and then starting again. What did you think about your decision to start smoking again six months ago - was there something specific that made you go back to it?

Yeah... I went out with friends to a bar, and we were all having drinks and laughing... and someone offered me a cigarette. I didn't even think twice about it. It was like my brain just shut off, and I was back to my old habits. And then, of course, one cigarette turned into a pack, and now I'm back to smoking 10-15 cigarettes a day.

I can see that social situations are really challenging for you, and it's clear that your friends' offer was a trigger that led you back to smoking. Can you tell me what it is about quitting that's important to you, and what would happen in your life if you were able to stay smoke-free?

I know it's bad for me, and I hate the way it makes me feel... guilty, anxious, and out of control. But at the same time, I really enjoy smoking. It's hard to explain, but it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy. And my friends all smoke, so it's hard to resist the temptation when we're together.

I can hear the internal conflict you're facing - on one hand, you hate the negative effects of smoking, but on the other hand, you enjoy the feeling it gives you. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "it's just something that makes me feel relaxed and happy"?
I guess so... I just don't know if I can do it. I feel like I've tried everything already.

You're feeling uncertain about quitting again, and it's understandable that you feel like you've tried everything before. 

What's the most difficult part about thinking about quitting that's holding you back right now?
Yeah, okay. I guess that sounds like a good place to start.
Let's focus on that situation where you're out with friends at a bar. What's the most challenging part of being in that situation for you?
Look, I know I smoke a lot. It's just something I do. I don't really see the big deal. My friends and family all smoke, so it's just a normal part of my life.
It sounds like tobacco has become a deeply ingrained habit for you, something you do without much thought. How long have you been smoking?

It's just something I've always done. I started in college, and it's just become a habit. I don't really think about it that much. And like I said, all my friends do it, so it's not like I'm going to quit just because you're telling me to.

It sounds like smoking has just become a routine part of your life, and you're not really thinking about the reasons behind it. Can you think of a specific situation or time of day when you usually smoke?

You don't understand. If I quit, my friends will think I'm lame or something. They'll stop inviting me to hang out. And what am I supposed to do instead of smoking when we're out at the bar? Just stand there and do nothing?

I can understand why you'd feel like quitting would change the way your friends see you, Alex. Can you tell me more about what's more important to you - keeping your friends' approval or taking care of your health?

I guess not, really. I mean, I've tried to quit a couple of times before, but it didn't last. And yeah, it would be nice not to have to worry about finding a place to smoke or having to leave the bar to go outside. But I just don't know if it's worth it.
Alex, it sounds like you're still unsure about whether quitting is worth it to you. What did you learn from your previous attempts to quit that might help you this time?
I guess so. But I'm not promising anything.

Alex, it's okay to take things one step at a time, and not promising anything is a big step in itself. Can you tell me what's holding you back from being more open to the idea of quitting, given that you're willing to consider it?

Honestly, it's been tough. I quit a few weeks ago, but I'm still struggling. The anxiety and irritability are killing me. I feel like I'm on edge all the time. And working at the pub doesn't help. Everyone's always smoking and drinking, and it's hard not to join in.

You feel really overwhelmed by the stress and social pressure at your job, and it's making it hard to stay quit. Can you think of a moment from your previous quit attempt that you felt like you were doing okay, even if it was just for a short time?

The hardest part is dealing with my emotions. I feel like I'm a different person when I'm not smoking. I'm short-tempered, and I just want to lash out at people. And the cravings are constant. I'll be in the middle of a shift, and suddenly I'll smell smoke and it's like my brain is screaming at me to go grab a cigarette.

Since we're in the planning phase, I'm going to gently introduce an idea to help you manage your stress and cravings. Have you ever tried taking a short walk outside during a craving to distract yourself and calm down?

Yeah, I've tried some stuff. Deep breathing, meditation... but it's hard to do that when you're in the middle of a busy shift. And to be honest, I'm not sure I believe in that stuff. I just feel like I need a cigarette to calm down.

You're feeling that deep breathing and meditation haven't worked for you in those high-pressure situations. Have you ever found that taking a short break, like stepping outside for some fresh air, helps you calm down and manage your cravings?
Hmm, that's a good point. If I'm not drinking, I might not be as tempted to smoke. But it's hard for me to imagine not having a drink after work. It's just what I do.
Would you be open to trying a simple routine to start your day in a healthier way, like drinking water or going for a short walk, to help reduce those morning cravings?
Yeah, I think I could do that. One shift a week, that's doable.
You've made a thoughtful decision — I'm confident you'll take care of yourself moving forward.
Yeah, that would be great. Thanks for listening and understanding. I feel like I'm not alone in this anymore.
You're not alone, and you're already taking powerful steps towards healing and growth.

Honestly, I'm feeling pretty discouraged. I quit for a few months, but I started again. It's just so hard. I enjoy smoking, you know? It's one of the few things that helps me relax and takes my mind off things.

You feel discouraged because you quit for a few months, but then started again. What was going on in your life when you quit for those few months, and how was your experience different when you relapsed?

Well, I like the taste, the smell... it's just a whole experience, you know? And it's social, too. I work in events, so I'm always around people who smoke. We'll take breaks together and chat while we're smoking. It's a way for me to connect with my colleagues and friends. Plus, it helps me focus and calm down when things get crazy.

I can see why smoking is such an enjoyable part of your social life and a way to cope with stress. Can you tell me who in your life knows about your struggles with quitting and might be willing to support you if you were to try again?

I don't know... I guess I just need to find something else that gives me that same feeling of relaxation and social connection. But it's hard, because nothing else seems to do the trick. And I'm worried about gaining weight if I quit. I've tried before and I ended up putting on a few pounds. That's just another stress I don't need.

It sounds like you're really struggling with finding a replacement for the social aspect of smoking. Have you ever considered taking a short walk with colleagues or friends during breaks, rather than smoking?

Actually, yeah... there are a few people I work with who don't smoke. Maybe I could try to connect with them more during breaks. That might help. But what about when I'm feeling stressed and overwhelmed? That's when I really need a cigarette.

You've mentioned that connecting with non-smoking colleagues might help during breaks, that's a good start. Would you be open to trying some short walks during stressful moments to see if that helps you manage your cravings?

Yeah, I've tried those things before. They help a bit, but they're not as... immediate, you know? A cigarette is just so quick and easy. But maybe if I had some other options that were just as easy... You know, this is actually making me think. Maybe I can try to find some other ways to cope with stress.

You're starting to see some possibilities for dealing with stress without smoking. Would you be willing to try carrying a small stress ball with you, like a little rubber ball, as a quick replacement for a cigarette when you feel anxious?

Honestly, I'm not really sure why I'm here. I mean, I know I should quit, but it's just so hard. I've been smoking for 15 years, and it's just become such a big part of my daily routine. I guess I'm just not really sure if I'm ready to give it up yet.

You're feeling unsure about quitting, and it's become a big part of your daily routine. What do you think would be the hardest part for you to give up - the physical act of smoking itself, or the emotional associations you've made with smoking over the years?

Well, I don't know... I just feel like I need cigarettes to cope with stress at work. I'm a marketing manager, and it can get really intense sometimes. Smoking just helps me calm down and focus. And to be honest, I've tried to quit before, but it never lasts. I feel like I'm just setting myself up for failure again.
Rachel, I hear that stress at work makes you feel like you need cigarettes to cope, and that's really tough to deal with. What is it about quitting that scares you the most?
Really? I didn't know that. I guess I just thought I was healthy enough to compensate for it.
